raw_case:
Swallowed drug-filled packet impaction and appendiceal neoplasm: Two rare causes of bowel obstruction detected in the same patient
Bowel obstruction, perforation and acute drug overdose due to packet rupture are known complication of “body-packing”. We present the case of a 47-year-old man, who ingested drug-filled packets with acute bowel obstruction. A CT scan revealed multiple hyperdense ovoid bodies along distended and fluid-filled small bowel loops and an infiltrating appendiceal tumor with proximal packet retention. Peritoneal carcinomatosis was also observed. Urgent laparotomy confirmed the radiological findings. This study aims to highlight the coincidence of 2 exceedingly rare causes of bowel obstruction in a single patient and emphasize the need for meticulous finding evaluation in acutely ill patients to avoid the perceptual errors associated with “satisfaction of search” bias.

Introduction

Intestinal adhesions, hernias and neoplasms (mainly colon or metastatic depots) are the leading causes of bowel obstruction. It is uncommon for 2 of these causes to coexist, producing a synergistic effect.This case presents intestinal obstruction due to the coincidence of 2 rare conditions: ingested drug-filled packets and an appendiceal neoplasm with peritoneal carcinomatosis.Radiological findings are discussed and correlated with those described in the literature.

Case presentation

A 47-year-old man presented to the emergency department 1 week after ingesting 88 cocaine-filled capsules, with symptoms of abdominal pain, vomiting, and abdominal distension. Despite the use of laxatives, he was only able to evacuate a few capsules, leading to progressive bowel obstruction. The patient was tachycardic, dehydrated, and had abdominal distension, but showed no symptoms of drug intoxication.A plain abdominal film revealed intestinal distension with some rounded bodies faintly visible in the pelvic area due to the lack of intestinal air (Fig. 1).On CT scan, a myriad of rounded, dense foreign bodies was observed along distended and fluid-filled jejunal and ileal loops, with only a few bodies had reached the colon (Fig. 2, Fig. 3, Fig. 4).Additionally, a 5 cm solid tumor was identified at the appendiceal base, infiltrating surrounding tissues and entrapping adjacent ileal loops, causing bowel obstruction along with the impacted foreign bodies. Right flank and omental solid implants and mesentery adenopathy were also present, indicating peritoneal and lymphatic spread (Fig. 3, Fig. 4).During laparotomy, an appendicular neoplasm infiltrating ileal loops and the parietal peritoneum was confirmed, causing drug-filled packet retention and bowel obstruction. Peritoneal carcinomatous implants were observed along the right side of the omentum and mesentery, with a peritoneal carcinomatosis index of 11.After surgical extraction of 78 drug-filled packets, right hemicolectomy, ileal-colonic anastomosis and epiplon resection, the patient recovered well, and awaited adjuvant therapy and HIPEC.Pathology revealed an infiltrating appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma with extension to the right colon and ileal loops, peritoneal spread, peritoneal pseudomyxoma formation and mesenteric adenopathy.

Discussion

Mules or body packers smuggle drugs across international borders by ingesting drug-filled packets [[1], [2], [3], [4]]. Cocaine is the most frequently carried drug, with Spain being a major entry point from South and Central America to Europe. A carrier typically ingests a large number of capsules, often near 100, each containing approximately 10 grams of drug powder encased in plastic or latex films. At the destination, the capsules are evacuated with the aid of laxative agents [[1], [2], [3], [4]].Although uncommon, bowel obstruction is a well-documented complication of body packing [[1], [2], [3], [4]], occurring in about 3% of cases in a cohort of 549 patients [2]. Other complications that can lead to an urgent laparotomy include perforation and acute intoxication due to packet rupture [1,2].When body packing is suspected, the initial radiological procedure is plain abdominal radiography [[1], [2], [3], [4]]. Drug packets appear as rounded or ovoid opacities along the intestinal tract [1,3,4]. Classically, there are some radiological signs, depending on the presence of intestinal air delineating the contour of the packets, that should be investigated [1,3,4]. As alternative to plain film, a previous study concluded that a low-dose CT exam is an effective diagnostic tool [4].In our patient's plain film, only faintly visible opacities were visible in the pelvis, probably due to the fluid that filled the bowel loops, masking the silhouette of the foreign bodies (Fig. 1).When plain film is inconclusive and the clinical suspicion remains high, or when complications are suspected, a CT scan should be performed [1,3,4].On CT, as in our case, drug packets appear as dense, ovoid bodies along the intestinal tract and are easily visible. Some of the plain film signs could also be detected (Fig. 2, Fig. 3, Fig. 4) [1,3,4]. Associated features such as signs of obstruction or perforation should be investigated.In our case, an additional pathological condition was present: a 5 cm appendiceal tumor with ileal infiltration and peritoneal spread, contributing to packet impaction and bowel obstruction. (Fig. 2, Fig. 3, Fig. 4).Appendiceal tumors are rare (<0.5% of gastrointestinal tumors) [5] and can be detected as incidental finding in appendicitis samples [[5], [6], [7]]. The majority consist of epithelial neoplasms and neuroendocrine tumors. Epithelial neoplasms are usually large and tend to infiltrate and spread to the peritoneum as classical carcinomatosis or as peritoneal pseudomyxoma (mucinous type), as in the present case [[5], [6], [7]]. Intestinal obstruction is a rare finding, present only in a 4% of mucinous tumors [5].In our patient, both conditions, presence of massive foreign intestinal content and the infiltrating neoplasm, led to the obstruction of the intestinal tract. Urgent laparotomy was performed due to obstructive symptoms and primarily because of the risk of an acute drug intoxication from packet rupture. Is unclear to determine the precise extent to which each entity contributed to the obstruction but is easy to understand that a synergistic effect was present.We found 2 reports in the literature where, as in the case presented, the coexistence of 2 pathologic conditions favored the intestinal obstruction. In both cases, a preexisting pathologic condition narrowing the intestinal lumen increased the risk for foreign body clogging [8,9].When evaluating an acutely ill patient exam, we must not be tempted to give up on the search when a positive finding, which explains the clinical suspicion, is found, to avoid the risk of underreading additional findings. This cause of perception errors is known as “satisfaction of search” [10].In our case, the presence of 2 exceedingly rare conditions, one of which was easy to detect and linked to the clinical data, could only be diagnosed through a systematic evaluation of the exam.

Declaration of generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process

During the preparation of this work the author(s) used Microsoft Copilot in order to improve grammatical quality. After using this tool/service, the author(s) reviewed and edited the content as needed and take(s) full responsibility for the content of the publication.

Patient consent

Informed consent was obtained from the individuals included in the case report.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information**: 47-year-old male.
- **Chief Complaint**: Abdominal pain, vomiting, and abdominal distension persisting for one week.
- **History of Present Illness**: Ingestion of 88 cocaine-filled capsules (body-packing) without successful complete evacuation despite laxatives, resulting in progressive bowel obstruction. 
- **Past Medical History**: Not mentioned.
- **Personal History**: Not discussed.
- **Family History**: Not discussed.
- **Physical Examination**: Tachycardia, dehydration, and abdominal distension noted. No signs of drug intoxication observed.
- **Ancillary Tests**: 
  - Plain abdominal radiograph showed intestinal distension with faintly visible rounded bodies in the pelvic area.
  - CT scan revealed numerous hyperdense, ovoid foreign bodies along distended small bowel loops, a 5 cm solid appendiceal tumor infiltrating surrounding tissues, and signs of peritoneal carcinomatosis (right flank and omental implants, mesenteric adenopathy).
  - Surgical findings confirmed appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma with ileal and right colon infiltration, along with peritoneal metastasis, forming pseudomyxoma peritonei. Retained drug-filled packets were removed surgically.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Body-packing with retained drug-filled packets**: Suspected based on the patient’s history of cocaine capsule ingestion, abdominal pain, vomiting, and distension. Confirmed by CT findings of hyperdense, ovoid foreign bodies along the intestinal tract.
   - *Rationale*: Body-packing is a known cause of bowel obstruction particularly when packets are retained or impacted.
2. **Appendiceal neoplasm causing bowel obstruction**:
   - *Rationale*: CT scan revealed a 5 cm appendiceal tumor infiltrating adjacent ileal loops and surrounding tissues, associated with peritoneal carcinomatosis, mesenteric adenopathy, and pseudomyxoma peritonei. These findings strongly supported appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma as a second synergistic cause of obstruction.
3. **Other potential causes of bowel obstruction (e.g., adhesions, hernias, other neoplasms)**: Ruled out as imaging and surgical findings strongly implicated the ingested drug packets and appendiceal tumor as the primary causes. There was no evidence of adhesions, hernias, or other obstructive lesions.
generate_case.final_diagnosis:
The patient was diagnosed with dual causes of bowel obstruction:
1. Retained cocaine-filled packets secondary to body-packing. 
2. Appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma with ileal and right colon infiltration, along with peritoneal carcinomatosis and pseudomyxoma peritonei.

The synergistic effect of the two conditions caused acute bowel obstruction. The diagnosis was confirmed via CT imaging and intraoperative findings.
generate_case.diagnosis_results:
1. Retained cocaine-filled packets causing bowel obstruction.
2. Appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma with peritoneal carcinomatosis.

results.content:
### Resoning:

**Step 1:** Start by reviewing the patient's chief complaints and history of present illness. The patient is a 47-year-old male who has been experiencing abdominal pain, vomiting, and abdominal distension for one week. He ingested 88 cocaine-filled capsules and was unable to completely evacuate them despite using laxatives, leading to progressive bowel obstruction.

**Step 2:** Consider the implications of ingesting drug-filled capsules. Body packing involves swallowing multiple capsules or balloons containing drugs like cocaine, heroin, or other illicit substances. The primary risk is the potential for rupture of these packets, leading to drug overdose and systemic toxicity. However, in this case, there are no signs of drug intoxication, which is reassuring.

**Step 3:** Focus on the presenting symptoms of abdominal pain, vomiting, and distension, which are classic signs of bowel obstruction. The failure to pass the capsules and the use of laxatives likely contributed to the obstruction.

**Step 4:** Review the physical examination findings: tachycardia and dehydration suggest that the patient is in some degree of shock or compensating for fluid loss due to vomiting. Abdominal distension further supports the diagnosis of bowel obstruction.

**Step 5:** Consider ancillary tests. The plain abdominal radiograph shows intestinal distension with faintly visible rounded bodies in the pelvic area, which could be the retained drug packets.

**Step 6:** The CT scan provides more detailed information: numerous hyperdense, ovoid foreign bodies along distended small bowel loops. Additionally, there is a 5 cm solid appendiceal tumor infiltrating surrounding tissues and signs of peritoneal carcinomatosis, including right flank and omental implants, and mesenteric adenopathy.

**Step 7:** The surgical findings confirm appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma with ileal and right colon infiltration, peritoneal metastasis forming pseudomyxoma peritonei, and the presence of retained drug-filled packets.

**Step 8:** Integrate all the information. The patient presented with symptoms of bowel obstruction due to retained drug packets, but the CT scan and surgical findings reveal an underlying malignancy—appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma with significant local invasion and peritoneal metastasis.

**Step 9:** Consider the timing and sequence of events. The patient's symptoms might have been precipitated by the ingestion of the drug packets, leading to obstruction, but the underlying cancer likely contributed to or exacerbated the obstruction.

**Step 10:** Given the presence of both the bowel obstruction due to drug packets and the advanced stage of appendiceal cancer with peritoneal metastasis, the differential diagnosis needs to account for both conditions.

**Step 11:** The surgical findings confirm the diagnosis of appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma with pseudomyxoma peritonei. This is a rare tumor that produces large amounts of mucin, leading to intraperitoneal deposition and associated symptoms.

**Step 12:** The patient's presentation is complicated by the presence of retained drug packets, which adds another layer of complexity to the management.

**Step 13:** Consider the potential for drug packet rupture and absorption, although in this case, there are no signs of drug intoxication.

**Step 14:** The primary diagnosis is appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma with pseudomyxoma peritonei and peritoneal metastasis, complicated by bowel obstruction due to retained drug packets.

**Step 15:** The differential diagnosis would include other causes of bowel obstruction, such as volvulus, hernia, or other malignancies, but given the surgical findings, appendiceal cancer is confirmed.

**Step 16:** Consider the prognosis and management options for appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma with pseudomyxoma peritonei. This typically involves cytoreductive surgery and hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC), but the feasibility depends on the extent of disease and the patient's overall condition.

**Step 17:** The presence of bowel obstruction due to drug packets may have delayed or complicated the diagnosis and treatment of the underlying cancer.

**Step 18:** In summary, the patient's primary issue is appendiceal mucinous adenocarcinoma with pseudomyxoma peritonei and peritoneal metastasis, presenting with bowel obstruction due to retained drug packets.

**Step 19:** The drug packets needed to be removed surgically, and the cancer requires a multidisciplinary approach to treatment.

**Step 20:** The final diagnosis should reflect both the malignant condition and the complication from the drug packets.

### Final Diagnosis:

**Appendiceal Mucinous Adenocarcinoma with Pseudomyxoma Peritonei and Bowel Obstruction due to Retained Drug-Packets**

---
